title: AI, Automation, and Your Career

modules.title: How Automation Has Always Changed Work
modules.sub: Tracing the historical arc from looms to algorithms
modules.description: Learners examine major waves of automation across history and identify recurring patterns in how technology displaces and creates jobs. By grounding today's AI moment in historical context, learners build a framework for separating panic from evidence-based analysis.

modules.title: Which Tasks AI Does Best Today
modules.sub: Mapping real AI capabilities against everyday job functions
modules.description: Learners break down jobs into discrete tasks and evaluate which categories — routine, predictive, generative — current AI systems handle reliably. They apply a task-decomposition tool to their own roles to assess realistic near-term exposure to automation.

modules.title: Industries Being Transformed Right Now
modules.sub: Examining live disruption across key economic sectors
modules.description: Learners investigate case studies from healthcare, logistics, finance, creative fields, and manufacturing to see how AI adoption is unfolding in practice. They compare projected disruption with actual hiring and displacement data to calibrate their own industry outlook.

modules.title: Skills That Remain Distinctly Human
modules.sub: Identifying durable capabilities machines cannot reliably replicate
modules.description: Learners explore the cognitive, emotional, and ethical capacities — such as contextual judgment, empathy, and creative synthesis — that current AI systems consistently struggle to replicate. They conduct a personal skills audit to identify where their own strengths align with these durable, high-value capabilities.

modules.title: Reskilling: Learning Alongside Machines
modules.sub: Designing a personal learning plan for an AI-integrated workplace
modules.description: Learners survey the landscape of reskilling pathways — from employer programs and community colleges to self-directed online learning — and evaluate each for fit, cost, and speed. They leave the module with a prioritized, actionable learning roadmap tailored to their career trajectory.

modules.title: Policy Responses to Job Displacement
modules.sub: Evaluating what governments and institutions can realistically do
modules.description: Learners analyze a range of policy interventions — including retraining subsidies, portable benefits, universal basic income pilots, and sectoral bargaining — using evidence from real-world implementations. They weigh trade-offs and form their own informed position on which policy levers are most likely to produce equitable outcomes.

modules.title: Entrepreneurs Thriving in an AI Economy
modules.sub: Spotting opportunities that emerge precisely because of automation
modules.description: Learners study entrepreneurs and small businesses that have built ventures by complementing, deploying, or serving AI-driven industries rather than competing against them. They apply an opportunity-mapping exercise to identify niches in their own field where human-AI collaboration creates new market space.

modules.title: Building a Career Strategy for Tomorrow
modules.sub: Synthesizing course insights into a forward-looking personal plan
modules.description: Learners integrate everything from previous modules — task exposure, durable skills, learning plans, and market signals — into a coherent, adaptive career strategy. They produce a concrete one-page career roadmap that anticipates multiple AI-driven scenarios and defines clear next steps they can take within thirty days.

synopsis: This course moves beyond headlines to give learners a clear-eyed, evidence-based look at which jobs AI is realistically automating, which it is augmenting, and what workers and policymakers can do about it. Whether you are a student, a professional, or a lifelong learner, you will leave with a practical framework for navigating the changing world of work.
rationale: A demand score of 6/10 and corpus similarity of 0.61 against a single existing module on jobs reveals that learners need a full structured course — not just a lesson — to understand the nuanced economic and career implications of automation.
nearest_existing: What AI Actually Does to Jobs
